source.definition: The part of the body that connects the head to the torso, through which the esophagus and trachea pass.

explanation.sourceText: The English word "neck" originates from the Old English 'hnecca', which is derived from a Proto-Germanic root referring to the nape or the connection between the head and body.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „neck” provine din vechiul englez 'hnecca', derivat dintr-o rădăcină proto-germanică care desemnează ceafa sau legătura dintre cap și corp.

target.definition: Partea corpului care leagă capul de trunchi, prin care trec esofagul și traheea.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian word "gât" comes from the Latin 'guttur', meaning 'throat' or 'pharynx', referring to the neck area.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul românesc „gât” provine din latină, din termenul 'guttur', care înseamnă 'gât' sau 'gâtlejul', referindu-se la zona gâtului.

examples.sourceText: He has a red neck from the cold wind.
examples.targetText: Are gâtul roșu din cauza vântului rece.

examples.sourceText: The neck connects the head with the body.
She is wearing a gold necklace.
examples.targetText: Ea poartă un colier de aur.

examples.sourceText: The doctor palpated his neck to check the lymph nodes.
examples.targetText: Doctorul i-a palpat gâtul pentru a verifica ganglionii limfatici.

learningTips.text: Remember the Romanian word "gât" by linking it to the English word "gate" because the neck acts like a gate between the head and body.
The Latin root 'guttur' sounds like 'gurgle', which happens in the throat—this helps you recall the meaning of "gât".
Think of a necklace ("colier") around your neck ("gât") to connect the Romanian word with a visible object you wear.
The word "gât" sounds a bit like 'got', so imagine you 'got' a sore neck to remember it.
